retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Indications -- FDA-labeled Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Parkinson disease: Drug-induced extrapyramidal reactions Influenza A: Prophylaxis and treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Administration -- FDA Approved
retrieved_docs.content: Amantadine is typically administered orally once daily or in divided doses depending on the indication. For the treatment of influenza A, amantadine 200 mg daily or 100 mg twice daily should be administered within 24 to 48 hours after the onset of symptoms and should be continued for 24 to 48 hours after the symptoms have resolved. For prophylaxis, patients should continue treatment for the entirety of the influenza season. For the treatment of Parkinson disease, amantadine is also given orally at 100 mg twice daily and increased to 200 mg twice daily as needed. Dosing should begin at 100 mg once daily for patients taking other parkinsonian drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Administration -- Non-FDA Approved
retrieved_docs.content: Huntington chorea: 100 mg orally three to four times daily. Multiple sclerosis-related fatigues: 100 mg orally twice daily, increasing to 200 mg twice daily as needed. Restless leg syndrome: 100 mg orally daily, increasing to 300 mg per day as needed. Traumatic brain injury: 100 mg orally twice daily, increasing to 200 mg twice daily as needed.

retrieved_docs.title: Amantadine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the proper indications for amantadine therapy. Describe the mechanism of action of amantadine. Review the potential adverse effects of amantadine. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team and how it can enhance patient care when dosing amantadine to improve patient outcomes for Parkinson patients. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

The formulations illustrate extensive variability in dosage strength, release profile (immediate, delayed, or extended‑release), route of administration (oral or injectable), and excipient composition (iron oxide, alginic acid), all of which clinicians must consider when selecting antimicrobial therapy or when managing patients with excipient sensitivities. Additionally, the cholinesterase inhibitor galantamine hydrobromide shares the same iron‑oxide inactive ingredient, underscoring the importance of recognizing common excipients across therapeutic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 313
retrieved_docs.content: The oral capsule and tablet formulations 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 16 mg Extended‑Release, 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 24 mg Extended‑Release, 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 8 mg Extended‑Release, 24 HR minocycline 135 mg Extended‑Release, 24 HR minocycline 45 mg Extended‑Release Tablet, doxycycline hyclate 50 mg Oral Capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 100 mg Oral Capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 150 mg Oral Capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 50 mg Oral Capsule, minocycline 100 mg Oral Capsule, minocycline 100 mg Oral Tablet, minocycline 50 mg Oral Capsule, minocycline 75 mg Oral Tablet, and moxifloxacin 400 mg Oral Tablet all share the same inactive ingredient, oxid železnato‑železitý. This common excipient links a range of extended‑release cholinesterase‑inhibitor products (galantamine) with several tetracycline‑class antibiotics (doxycycline, minocycline) and a fluoroquinolone antibiotic (moxifloxacin). Recognizing that oxid železnato‑železitý is present across these diverse medications is important for patient safety, particularly for individuals with hypersensitivity or contraindications to iron‑based compounds, and it informs prescribing and counseling practices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 803
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release oral capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, and 24 mg), minocycline extended‑release oral capsules (135 mg) and tablets (45 mg, 50 mg, 75 mg, and 100 mg), doxycycline hyclate oral capsules (50 mg and 100 mg), doxycycline monohydrate oral capsules (50 mg and 75 mg), and moxifloxacin oral tablets (400 mg) all share the same inactive ingredient, oxid železnato‑železitý (iron oxide). The consistent inclusion of this iron‑oxide excipient across a range of antibiotic, anticholinesterase, and fluoroquinolone products—regardless of dosage strength or dosage form (capsule versus tablet)—highlights its widespread use in pharmaceutical formulations. Awareness of this common excipient is important for clinicians and patients who may have sensitivities, allergies, or contraindications related to iron‑oxide compounds, as it may affect tolerability or safety across multiple prescribed medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: M [Preparations] represents a class of pharmaceutical formulations that is closely linked to a variety of active agents through both parent‑compound and derivative relationships. As a parent compound, M [Preparations] gives rise to the drugs metopimazine, mefenytoin, and minocycline hydrochloride, indicating that these medications are formulated from or chemically derived from the preparation. Conversely, M [Preparations] is itself derived from—or has as parent compounds—a broad spectrum of substances, including medium‑chain triglycerides, medronate disodium, melphalan flufenamide, melphalan hydrochloride, metaxalone, mitoxantrone hydrochloride, moroxydine, moxifloxacin hydrochloride, and moxifloxacin hydrochloride monohydrate. This dual role underscores the preparation’s function as both a source material for certain drugs and a formulation that incorporates a range of precursor compounds, reflecting its importance in drug development, formulation design, and therapeutic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 702
retrieved_docs.content: The oral dosage forms 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 16 mg extended‑release capsule, 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 24 mg extended‑release capsule, and 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide 8 mg extended‑release capsule all contain the inactive ingredient oxid železnato‑železitý. Likewise, the extended‑release products 24 HR minocycline 135 mg capsule and 24 HR minocycline 45 mg tablet, as well as the immediate‑release formulations doxycycline monohydrate 100 mg capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 50 mg capsule, doxycycline monohydrate 75 mg capsule, minocycline 100 mg capsule, minocycline 50 mg tablet, minocycline 75 mg capsule, and minocycline 75 mg tablet, also list oxid železnato‑železitý as an inactive component. In addition, tetracycline hydrochloride 250 mg oral capsule includes oxid železnato‑železitý among its excipients. This pattern shows that oxid železnato‑železitý is a common excipient used across multiple galantamine and tetracycline‑class antibiotic products, suggesting that clinicians should be aware of this shared ingredient when evaluating patients for potential excipient sensitivities or allergies, and that formulators rely on it for consistent drug delivery in both extended‑release and immediate‑release oral preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other psychostimulants and nootropics” comprises agents such as aniracetam, acylcarnitine, adrafinil (a pro‑drug of modafinil and α‑adrenergic agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, fipexide, nizofenone, oxiracetam, pipradrol, prolintane, temgicoluril (structurally related to mebikar) and others, classified as psychoanaleptics used for ADHD and cognitive enhancement. Within this group, centrally acting sympathomimetics—including atomoxetine, methamfetamin, methylfenidát and modafinil—are employed for the same indications. Antidepressants form a distinct subclass of psychoanaleptics and are therapeutically linked to the sodium‑valproate salt class; representative members include citalopram, lithium, bupropion (and its hydrobromide and hydrochloride salts, also a smoking‑cessation agent), desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, esketamine, sidnocarb, sulpiride, atomoxetine, fipexide, iproniazid (also listed under antituberculotics for its historic MAOI activity), klorgylin, moklobemid, nialamid, rolapram, tofisopam, methylfenidát, pipradrol and prolintane, with trazodone serving as the prototypical parent concept. Important safety interactions are noted: citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride and trazodone are contraindicated with the oxazolidinone antibiotic linezolid because of serotonin‑syndrome risk; citalopram use may be complicated by alcohol dependence; and bupropion and its salts are contraindicated in patients experiencing withdrawal syndrome, underscoring the need to evaluate dependence‑related conditions before prescribing. Tofisopam is specifically indicated for the treatment of withdrawal syndrome, highlighting its role in managing substance‑discontinuation symptoms. The trade name Antituberkulotika refers to an isoniazid 100 mg/mL injectable solution used for Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection, illustrating the therapeutic application of antitubercular agents such as iproniazid within this context.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid železnato‑železitý is a widely used inactive excipient that appears in numerous oral capsule and tablet products, including 24‑hour galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg), 24‑hour minocycline extended‑release capsules (45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg), standard‑release minocycline capsules and tablets (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg), doxycycline hyclate capsules (50 mg, 100 mg) and injection (100 mg), doxycycline hyclate delayed‑release tablets (50 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg), doxycycline monohydrate capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), and tetracycline hydrochloride capsules (250 mg, 500 mg). All of the listed minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, tetracycline hydrochloride, and tigecycline (50 mg injection) products belong to the tetracycline antibiotic class, providing broad‑spectrum antibacterial activity for a variety of bacterial infections. These agents differ in dosage strength, release profile (immediate, delayed, or extended release), and route of administration (oral capsule, oral tablet, or injection), allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to infection severity and patient‑specific factors. Awareness of the shared pharmacologic class aids in selecting appropriate tetracycline therapy, optimizing dosing regimens, and recognizing potential excipient‑related sensitivities or adverse reactions associated with oxid železnato‑železitý.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release oral capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, and 24 mg) all contain the inactive ingredient oxid železnato‑železitý, as do minocycline extended‑release oral capsules (45 mg and 135 mg) and the minocycline extended‑release oral tablet (45 mg). Minocycline oral tablets of 100 mg also include oxid železnato‑železitý. Doxycycline hyclate oral capsules (50 mg and 100 mg) and doxycycline monohydrate oral capsules (75 mg, 100 mg, and 150 mg) share the same inactive component. Additionally, levofloxacin 500 mg oral tablets contain oxid železnato‑železitý. The presence of oxid železnato‑železitý across these diverse oral formulations—spanning anticholinesterase agents, tetracycline antibiotics, and fluoroquinolone antibiotics—highlights it as a common excipient. Awareness of this shared inactive ingredient is important for clinicians when evaluating potential excipient sensitivities or allergies in patients and when selecting alternative therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: Galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release oral capsules of 8 mg, 16 mg, and 24 mg, minocycline extended‑release oral capsules of 45 mg and 135 mg, minocycline oral tablets of 50 mg, 75 mg, and 100 mg, minocycline oral capsules of 50 mg, doxycycline hyclate 50 mg oral capsule, doxycycline monohydrate oral capsules of 75 mg and 100 mg, and tetracycline hydrochloride 500 mg oral capsule all share the same inactive ingredient, oxid železnato‑železitý. The repeated inclusion of oxid železnato‑železitý across these galantamine, minocycline, doxycycline, and tetracycline products indicates that it is a common excipient used in formulations of both a cholinesterase inhibitor (galantamine) and several tetracycline‑class antibiotics. Awareness of this shared component is important for clinicians and pharmacists, especially when managing patients who may have hypersensitivity or contraindications to oxid železnato‑železitý, as it could affect multiple prescribed medications simultaneously.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: Oxid železnato‑železitý, an iron‑based excipient, is incorporated in a wide range of oral dosage forms, including the 24‑hour extended‑release capsules of galantamine hydrobromide (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg) and numerous tetracycline‑class antibiotics such as all extended‑release minocycline capsules (45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg), minocycline tablets and capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg), doxycycline hyclate capsules (50 mg, 100 mg) and delayed‑release tablets (50 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg), doxycycline monohydrate capsules (50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), tetracycline hydrochloride capsules (250 mg, 500 mg), and the tetracycline‑hydrochloride 500 mg capsule. The repeated use of this iron‑derived compound suggests it provides matrix stability, binding, or flow properties, and clinicians should consider possible sensitivities or contraindications to iron compounds when prescribing these products. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 58
retrieved_docs.content: The community identifies a broad group of pharmaceutical products that share the inactive excipient oxid železnato‑železitý (iron‑oxide), including Galantamine hydrobromide extended‑release oral capsules (8 mg, 16 mg, 24 mg), levofloxacin 500 mg oral tablet, all formulations of minocycline (extended‑release capsules and tablets 45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg; immediate‑release capsules and tablets 50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg; 24 HR extended‑release capsules/tablets 45 mg, 90 mg, 135 mg; 50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg capsules/tablets), doxycycline hyclate (oral capsules 50 mg, 100 mg; injection 100 mg; delayed‑release tablets 50 mg, 150 mg, 200 mg) and doxycycline monohydrate (oral capsules 50 mg, 75 mg, 100 mg, 150 mg), as well as tetracycline hydrochloride oral capsules 250 mg and 500 mg. All of these agents belong to the tetracycline antibiotic class—minocycline, doxycycline hyclate, doxycycline monohydrate, tetracycline hydrochloride, and tigecycline 50 mg injection—characterized by inhibition of bacterial protein synthesis and use in diverse infections. The presence of the iron‑oxide excipient across multiple dosage forms and strengths highlights its widespread role as a coloring or filler component, and clinicians must consider it when assessing patient sensitivities, performing medication reconciliation, or selecting appropriate therapy. The classification also clarifies that Galantamine hydrobromide, a cognitive‑enhancing acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, shares the same excipient but is pharmacologically distinct from the tetracycline antibiotics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 106
retrieved_docs.content: Antidepressants are classified within the broader psychoanaleptics domain and represent a heterogeneous therapeutic class that includes classic serotonergic agents (citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride), norepinephrine‑dopamine reuptake inhibitors (bupropion hydrobromide, bupropion hydrochloride), NMDA‑receptor antagonists (esketamine), mood‑stabilizers (lithium), atypical antipsychotics with antidepressant use (sulpiride), stimulants (methylphenidate, atomoxetine, methylfenidát, modafinil, methamfetamin), and other compounds such as aniracetam, moklobemide, klorgylin, fipexide, deanol, nialamid, rolipram, and tofisofam. Trazodone is identified as a parent concept for antidepressants, and both trazodone and the antibiotic linezolid share a contraindication, while linezolid is also contraindicated with citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, and duloxetine hydrochloride, underscoring the need for careful medication reconciliation. Citalopram’s efficacy may be

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 599
retrieved_docs.content: The oral formulations of 24 HR galantamine hydrobromide (16 mg, 24 mg, and 8 mg extended‑release capsules), 24 HR minocycline (135 mg and 45 mg extended‑release capsules), doxycycline hyclate (100 mg capsule), doxycycline monohydrate (100 mg and 75 mg capsules), levofloxacin (750 mg tablet), minocycline (50 mg capsule, 50 mg tablet, and 75 mg capsule), and tetracycline hydrochloride (250 mg and 500 mg capsules) all share the same inactive ingredient, oxid železnato‑železitý. This common excipient is present across a range of antibiotic and cognitive‑enhancing products, indicating that patients who are sensitive or allergic to this iron‑based compound may react to any of these medications. Recognizing the shared presence of oxid železnato‑železitý is important for clinicians when reviewing medication histories, counseling patients about potential excipient‑related adverse effects, and selecting alternative formulations for individuals with known intolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: M [Preparations] is a class of pharmaceutical formulations that is linked to a variety of parent compounds and active agents. It is derived from several parent substances, including medium chain triglycerides, melphalan flufenamide, melphalan hydrochloride, mitoxantrone hydrochloride, and moxifloxacin hydrochloride (both the standard form and the monohydrate variant, with a minor typographical variant “moxifloxacin hydrochlorid” referring to the same compound). At the same time, M [Preparations] serves as the parent formulation for the active drugs metopimazine and minocycline hydrochloride. This dual role illustrates how M [Preparations] functions both as a carrier built upon excipients such as medium chain triglycerides and as a delivery vehicle for specific therapeutic agents. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for formulation scientists and clinicians, as the choice of parent compounds influences the stability, bioavailability, and overall therapeutic effectiveness of the resulting medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1168
retrieved_docs.content: M [Preparations] represents a collection of pharmaceutical formulations that encompass several specific agents, including metopimazine, methylergonovin, minocycline hydrochloride, and mivakurium‑chlorid, each of which is directly generated as a preparation within this group. At the same time, the M [Preparations] family is derived from a set of parent compounds, namely melphalan flufenamide, melphalan hydrochloride, metaxalone, mitoxantrone hydrochloride, moxifloxacin hydrochlorid, and moxifloxacin hydrochloride monohydrate, indicating that these parent drugs serve as the chemical or therapeutic basis for the preparations. In addition, the agent methylergonovin carries a specific safety consideration: it is contraindicated when used concomitantly with drugs belonging to the macrolide class, highlighting the need for clinicians to avoid combining methylergonovin with macrolide antibiotics to prevent adverse interactions. This integrated view of the relationships among the preparations, their parent compounds, and the contraindicated drug class informs both formulation development and safe prescribing practices.

retrieved_docs.content: and nootropics in ATC” subgroup comprises acyl‑carnitine, adrafinil (an adrenergic α‑receptor agonist), cytidine‑diphosphate‑choline, deanol (formulated with hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL topical cream), fipexide, nizofenone, oxiracetam, pipradrol, prolintane, and temgicoluril; a noted relationship between mebikar and temgicoluril suggests possible interaction or co‑formulation. Safety considerations include contraindications between the MAOI‑like antibiotic linezolid and serotonergic antidepressants citalopram, desvenlafaxine succinate, duloxetine hydrochloride, and also linezolid with trazodone, due to the risk of serotonin syndrome. Sulpiride, primarily an antipsychotic, appears in an “isa” relationship to antidepressants, representing a classification inconsistency. Overall, many of these drugs belong to multiple therapeutic categories—psychostimulants, nootropics, anti‑dementia agents, antidepressants, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics—and recognizing their shared mechanisms,
